sentences.text: My name is Matthew Guttman.
M-A-T-T-H-E-W G-U-T-T-M-A-N.
My name at birth was Matei, but it's same thing.
Guttman is the same thing.
M-A-T-E-I.
That was Romanian, because I was born in Czechoslovakia.
In Bratislava, former Czechoslovakia.
I was born May the 5th, 1920-- 19-- May the 25th, 1920, excuse me.
No, the same name I had before.
I was born in Bratislava.
Eight months later, my parents moved to Transylvania, because Transylvania became Romania.
And all his relatives were there.
Even they were born there, except me, because I was born in Czechoslovakia.
So they left home to their parents' town.
They moved into a small, little town.
[INAUDIBLE].
That was 174 numbers.
The whole town had 174 numbers, and the only Jew in town.
My father was a blacksmith.
He made a very meager living.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We were about-- I was the only one then.
Slowly by slowly, after two siblings later, my mother was a housewife, God rest her in peace.
Yes, about two siblings later, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it didn't go better.
It was only worse.
It came the time that's really, we didn't have so-- too much to eat.
I mean, food was missing on the table.
I remember at night, my mother used to say, 'Matthew, please, leave some bread for tomorrow.' I was hungry, because there weren't really exactly the all essentials.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when I was eight, my father took me away, about 35 kilometers from there.
It was a town called Ileanda [INAUDIBLE].
There were about 40 Jewish families.
I had to go there to start to learn cheder.
And [INAUDIBLE] through Hebrew school and Romanian.
But I had nobody except my father organized, I should [INAUDIBLE] days.
If you don't know what that means, I'm going to explain it to you.

Today, like Monday, you gave me a day [INAUDIBLE] by you, meaning through the-- half a year, through the season what I'm going to be there, Monday is going to be by you, and tomorrow is going to be by him, and the third day or tomorrow is going to be by somebody else, and so on.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were good days.
I mean, what they had, they gave me enough to eat.
And there were bad days when they were themselves not too rich, and they could not afford to give me.
And I was hungry like a dog.
It used to be, for example, I used to come to eat lunch.
I used to have one-hour lunch from school.
I had to go-- she ask me, the lady of the house, 'Please, Matthew, if you are not going to the ritual slaughter of the kill a chicken, we won't have no [? filet ?] for Shabbos.
I won't-- won't have no meat for Saturday.
Do me a favor and go?' So what can I say?
After all, she was my day-giver.
I was running to the slaughter.
Until everything, after I came back, the hour was over.
And I ran back without lunch, without nothing at all.
The Hebrew teacher didn't want to believe me, because I was late.
So he was beating the hell out of me, because he thought that I was playing around.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So until everything-- by my father, my father was a pious sort of Jew.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He expected no more, no less that his son is going to be a rabbi.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yes.
[SIGHS] But whatever I will do, whatever I won't do, but I have to learn.
And life didn't go too well for me.
When I was 13 years old, I was bar mitzvah.
I couldn't take it no more.
I ran away from this town here.
My father shouldn't know where my-- my whereabouts.
And I want to learn a trade.
I didn't want to be hungry.
I couldn't take the hunger, and I couldn't take the sufferings.
I didn't see no future.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 1933, the economic situation was getting bad, because even here in United States, how much I understand, it wasn't so-- so good.
But there, it was horribly worse.
And I couldn't-- just couldn't take it no more.
Because I was going around hungry, and in wintertime, I wasn't dressed for the cold occasion, and so on.
Finally, I ran away to another town where my father won't know about it, except my mother, rest her in peace.
I let her know-- not-- my father shouldn't have known.
Should he have known where I am, he would have come.
And by my ears, he would schlep me back to go back to [?
Yeshiva ?] to learn.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I learned the trade, the fur trade there.
Right away, my situation improved to 100%.
At least he gave me food to eat.
I learned the trade for two and a half years.
With-- in the house, in the-- by my-- by my boss.
Of course, in the beginning, I had to take more care of the children and do more housework than learn trades.
But in the meantime, it was food.
It was clothing-- he gave me clothing.
Poorly, but surely, better than I had before.
I wasn't hungry no more.
And then I started to learn the trade.
Two and a half years later, I was liberated with a permit that I am a master.
And I was earning money, and everything was OK.
But now I was-- it was five years later when I ran away from school that I went home.
I permitted myself that luxury that even my father couldn't have no more the authority over me, because I am making already a living.
And that's the way I went home.
That, and everything was going on, it was flowing.
Life was flowing until around-- and that was until around 1940.
I was already 18 years old, because I was born in '20, 1920.
So I came home.
I-- I saw the siblings, you know, what I never knew that they exist.
My mother used to write me that 'Now we have another little brother,' 'Now we have another little sister,' and so on.
We were seven children by my parents.
My father, rest him in peace-- he couldn't make a big living when I come, because he was ruptured and [? herniate ?] on both-- on both sides.
Whenever he used to come home from work, he has-- he has to lay down and to fix himself, because he was herniated.
He was ruptured on both sides, so he couldn't make a very big-- a good living.
So anyway, I came home.
My father was happy, he told me, to see me.
I was grown up already.
I was well-dressed and everything.
And he saw that I am no more going to be a rabbi, because [INAUDIBLE] I have already a-- I was a haircut, and so on, and so on.
But I was well-dressed, and everything was right, everything was flowing.
Life was flowing poorly, but surely.
I mean, until 1940, in two more years.
No, no.
I went back, and I was ready.
Listen, ?] I couldn't stay, really, in this little town.
I didn't have nothing what to do there.
I went back, and I came back, and so on.
Finally, in 1940, the Hungarians [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] occupied our part of Transylvania.
And overnight, it became Hungary.
This Jewish situation worsened all the way.
What we had before by the Romanians wasn't so bad, because-- as a matter of fact, it was good.
They were not such big anti-Semites like they were here.
They came in right away with the Jew, not Romanian-- you should-- whatever you see him, you should kill him.
With that kind of motto, they came in.
[SIGHS] I saw that life-- it wasn't going on the way it's supposed to.
And I couldn't-- I came to my father's town, but there, I couldn't exist.
Finally, I left to a big city now.
Because I was in Hungary, I left to Budapest, the capital of Hungary.
Here, as a Jew, I got lost between the people.
There were so many people.
There came a time, you know, when, they were-- wherever you came in from to looking for a job, you couldn't get a job because you were a Jew.
You went in a restaurant, it's-- there's written, 'Jews and dogs are not allowed.'
So I came to Budapest.
I bought myself false alien papers, Gentile papers.
My name was [?
Matyus ?] Andre, a Hungarian name, a real Hungarian name.
And now these papers, I got myself a job.
I was working, and everything was quite all right until 1943.
I'd been there 1941 until 1943.
Working, yes, as a Gentile.
And I was accepted everywhere, you know?
Maybe there were some of them-- because, you know, those-- they were smelling, you know, like-- like German shepherds.
They were smelling you, oh, that you are a Jew.
There were some of them, they were thinking.
But thinking is not enough.
That was until--
No, no.
Because I was working in an army war factory.
But forget about Jews working there.
But I smoothed myself through, and everything was all right.
And it came like Saturday noon, we went out of the factory, so everybody went to the big saloon.
And for the foreman, they used to buy him beer, you know.
They were buying, drinking beer.
And I was one among them.
I didn't want to remain, you know, on the sidelines.
Because then they are going to smell you out, that you are-- it's not right.
Everything was going until one day, I went to a movie house.
It was-- they were giving a picture, a very, very anti-Semitic picture, a German picture called [GERMAN].
It was a horror-- They were jumping out, these Hungarians, you know, in the movie. 'Kill the Jew!
Kill the Jew!
Kill the Jew!' That was horror.
I just wanted to see what in the hell is going on.
Because I read in the papers, you know, the big-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] things, what they are doing.
Of course, 11 o'clock at night, [? we ?] went out of the movie.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was a big raid.
The police made a big raid around the movie.
All around the place, all around it.
It was a big raid.
And, of course, everybody had to ID himself, identifying himself.
Of course, when my next came, we had to stay in the line, you know.
And my next came, I came to give him the papers.
He says, [? 'Matyas ?] Andre?' He looked at me.
He saw I have a [? cockeyed ?] nose.
He says, 'That couldn't be possible.' I'm telling you, like German shepherds-- 'That couldn't be possible [INAUDIBLE] [?
Matyas, ?] [?
Matyas ?] Andre.' They took me to the corner, they let down my pants, identified my circumcision, and I was arrested on the place.
In 1943 in spring.
I was taken to an army barrack in Budapest.
From there, another 50 kilometers, there was a big, big army place.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they put us in-- into [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] companies.
A war company of soldiers contains for 224 people.
So we were the same thing-- force us forced labor camp, in our own clothes.
And we-- in the beginning, we were working in a vineyard on the place.
It wasn't so bad.
Oh yeah, about 40 kilometers.
Yeah, [INAUDIBLE].
From there, there was a very anti-Semitic colonel.
But he had a-- a converted Jew, but converted to Catholicism.
They were also persecuted.
You may have been converted to Catholicism, your children were Catholics.
Or your father was born Catholic-- until the third generation, you were still a Jew.
That was the law-- anti-Semitic laws.
Until the third generation, you were still Jewish.
So then here was a man with a white arm band on the left.
We were wearing yellow bands on, because we were Jews.
He was wearing a white one, because he was half-Gentile, half-Jew.
And this one, he believed he was somehow a friend to the-- to this colonel.
But colonel was anti-Semitic.
He was a nice Jew, the same thing like he was-- like he would have been before.
And what he could, he did for people.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One day, we heard rumors that we are are going to get taken-- they are going to take us to the Ukraine in the battle line to work alongside there on the front.
To carry ammunition, dig trenches and bunkers, and so on, and so on.
And that was the truth.
Horrible, horrible.
The working conditions-- they were very bad, but the-- the beatings were even worse.
For every little thing, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they tied you up [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to the back, with your hands and a rope, they tied it up to the-- tied up your feet.
And then they pulled up the rope, and you were on the point of your finger-- toes.
And that high, they pulled you up, that a newspaper, they could have [? pulled ?] through to your feet, because it means you had to [? lift ?] only on your nerves.
And that would be, you know, for two hours, depends for how long you were sentenced.
If you-- if you-- if you fainted, they took a pail of cold water, they throw it over you, and back up again.
The sentence had to be carried out.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They took us to the battle line.
And here, I'm talking-- you know, it is easier said than done.
Because we were Jews, we weren't so much in the necessity, you know, to go to the battle line, than the soldiers, the fighting soldiers.
So the train with the soldiers-- they had to let them go through.
And they [INAUDIBLE] deadline until everything [INAUDIBLE] during the night.
Then we started moving, and then we went all night long.
How long did it take to get to the Ukraine?
Oh, we stopped a few places before.
I don't know.
We arrived there about-- we arrived in Briansk, Ukraine.
There, a lot of, especially [INAUDIBLE] Hungarian people know about it, but this was the biggest punishment what they could give the Jews.
They were dying like flies there.
Dysentery, ?] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] all kinds of-- After being there, we arrived there, they put us to pick up landmines what the Russians left over.
It was already in the fall of 1943.
We picked up landmines.
We had dogs what they smelled out the mine, where it was.
Then we had to go-- they told us what to do.
We had to go to unscrew to take away the-- the earth from around it, and to unscrew the two screws where they really detonated the mine.
Where that-- that was the wire what really start it up.
So we throw away the fuses.
And once a week, Sunday, we exploded them, the land mines.
Everything was going all right.
How many people did you work with?
How many people were in your group?
With ?] 224 people, that was the-- when we arrived there, we were 224 people.
But one night, the partisans, the Russian partisans came.
And they made a raid over the place in the woods there.
They surrounded the whole area, and they started shooting.
We were-- we had a captain, a Hungarian captain.
And we had about 10 soldiers-- sergeants, and so on, and so on, corporals.
[INAUDIBLE] they were taking care of us, like I told you.
They were hanging us by our hands, or beating the hell out of us.
And then they were giving us away to work with another detachment, sometimes with Germans, sometimes with the Hungarian attachments, and they were beating us.
We had to take, for instance, digging trenches or bunkers.
We had to chop wood from the-- from the trees-- clean them off.
Two guys-- they were giving three-- two guys, the two ends of the-- of the log, and the short one in the middle.
That means he couldn't reach under shoulder nothing, because he was the short one.
Everything was-- And then they were beating you from behind to run uphill with the log.
How many times the log fell down, the people fell down.
And the got butchered, or killed, or whatever.
All Jewish.
All Jewish.
There were only Jews, nothing else.
All- all with the yellow armbands, your own clothes.
And now the fall was coming, and the cold there was cold like hell.
And we weren't dressed for the occasion.
We weren't dressed [INAUDIBLE].
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Finally, the Russian offensive started.
They started pushing the Germans.
We were running.
We were running-- a mixture of Germans, Gestapo, SS, Hungarians, Romanians.
We were running like sheep.
With the same group.
We were running and stopping when the Russian front-- when the Russians stabilized themselves-- I mean, the offensive ran out of-- of strength, we stopped.
And they were digging new trenches, and new things, the same thing, you know.
Because we should-- they should be-- And when the-- the front line came near, then we had to take boxes with ammunition.
And everyone on the shoulder, you know, 250 rounds on your shoulder-- the box on the shoulder, you know, to schlep up, you know, into the trenches for the soldiers.
And sometimes, the soldier himself shot you.
They kill you, they kill you.
So you were, you know, like a dog.
They aimed like on a dog, you know, point blank.
Nothing, really.
Where did just sleep when you were--?
In the-- in the bunkers.
It was bunkers.
So what I wanted to say before-- I'm skipping from one place to the other.
The partisans came, and--
--they captured all the Hungarian soldiers.
The captain was in town somewhere.
He was away.
And they took the soldiers right away in the front of us.
They killed them.
From us, they just took away our knapsacks.
And we would remain now only with the clothes that we wore on, we had on ourselves.
But it started-- the Russian offensive started, and we were running.
